Glossary:
# Glossary — Computer Systems

> A quick-reference for terminology used throughout the Computer Systems curriculum. Each term links to the note where it's explained in depth.

## A

**ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit) — The part of the CPU that performs integer arithmetic and logic operations. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**AMAT** (Average Memory Access Time) — `hit_time + miss_rate × miss_penalty`. The expected time for a memory access given cache hierarchy. [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**API** (Application Programming Interface) — A contract between software components. [[Hardware/09 — DMA & Bus Mastering]]

**ASLR** (Address Space Layout Randomization) — Security technique that randomizes the base address of the stack, heap, and libraries. [[Hardware/15 — Hardware Security]]

## B

**BIOS** — Basic Input/Output System, the legacy firmware interface for x86 PCs. [[Hardware/10 — Boot Process — BIOS, UEFI & Bootloader]]

**Branch Predictor** — Hardware that guesses whether a conditional branch will be taken. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Bus** — A communication system that transfers data between components. [[Hardware/09 — DMA & Bus Mastering]]

## C

**Cache** — Small, fast memory close to the CPU that stores copies of frequently-accessed data. [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**Cache Coherence** — The protocol that ensures all caches see a consistent view of memory. [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**Cache Line** — The unit of data transfer in a cache (typically 64 bytes). [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy]]

**Calling Convention** — The rules for how functions receive arguments and return values. [[System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics]]

**CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) — CPU design philosophy with many complex instructions (e.g., x86). [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Context Switch** — Saving the state of one process/thread and restoring another. [[Concurrency/01 — Processes & Threads]]

**CPI** (Cycles Per Instruction) — Average clock cycles per instruction. A measure of CPU efficiency. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

## D

**DMA** (Direct Memory Access) — Hardware that transfers data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement. [[Hardware/09 — DMA & Bus Mastering]]

**DRAM** (Dynamic RAM) — Memory that stores each bit as a capacitor charge; needs periodic refresh. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/01 — DRAM Cells & Memory Physics]]

## E

**Endianness** — The byte order of multi-byte values: little-endian (LSB first) or big-endian (MSB first). [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness]]

## F

**False Sharing** — Two threads modify different variables that happen to share a cache line, causing unnecessary coherence traffic. [[Concurrency/07 — Synchronization Patterns]]

**Fragmentation** — Wasted memory: internal (within allocated blocks) or external (between blocks). [[Hardware/06 — Memory Allocation]]

**Frame** (Stack Frame) — The region of the stack belonging to a single function call. [[System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics]]

## G

**GPU** (Graphics Processing Unit) — A massively parallel processor optimized for graphics and compute workloads. [[Hardware/11 — GPU Architecture]]

## H

**Huge Page** — A memory page larger than 4 KB (typically 2 MB or 1 GB), reducing TLB pressure. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]]

## I

**Interrupt** — A signal to the CPU that requires immediate attention. [[Hardware/12 — Interrupts & Exceptions]]

**IPC** (Instructions Per Cycle) — Inverse of CPI; how many instructions complete per clock cycle. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**ISA** (Instruction Set Architecture) — The abstract interface between software and hardware (e.g., x86-64, ARM, RISC-V). [[Hardware/03 — Assembly Language]]

## K

**Kernel** — The core of the operating system running in privileged mode. [[System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]

## L

**Little-Endian** — Byte order where the least significant byte is stored first. Used by x86. [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness]]

## M

**MMU** (Memory Management Unit) — Hardware that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses. [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]]

**mmap** — Unix system call that maps files or devices into memory. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]]

**Moore's Law** — The observation that transistor density doubles roughly every 2 years. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Mutex** (Mutual Exclusion) — A synchronization primitive that ensures only one thread accesses a resource. [[Concurrency/04 — Data Races & Mutexes]]

## N

**NUMA** (Non-Uniform Memory Access) — Memory architecture where access latency depends on which CPU socket the memory is attached to. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/04 — Huge Pages, NUMA & Boot Process]]

**NVMe** (Non-Volatile Memory Express) — A high-performance protocol for SSDs over PCIe. [[Hardware/Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]]

## O

**Out-of-Order Execution** — The ability of a CPU to execute instructions in non-sequential order to maximize throughput. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

## P

**Page** — A fixed-size block of virtual memory (typically 4 KB). [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]]

**Page Fault** — An exception raised when a virtual address has no physical mapping. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/03 — Page Faults, mmap & Swap]]

**Page Table** — A data structure that maps virtual pages to physical frames. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/02 — Page Tables & TLB]]

**PCIe** (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) — High-speed serial bus standard. [[Hardware/Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]]

**Pipeline** — A CPU design where instruction processing is divided into stages, allowing multiple instructions to overlap. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**Process** — An instance of a running program with its own virtual address space. [[Concurrency/01 — Processes & Threads]]

**Protection Ring** — A privilege level for code execution (Ring 0 = kernel, Ring 3 = user). [[System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]

## R

**RAID** (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) — Combining multiple drives for performance or reliability. [[Hardware/08 — RAID]]

**RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) — CPU design with simple, uniform instructions (e.g., ARM, RISC-V). [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

## S

**SATA** (Serial ATA) — Interface standard for connecting storage devices. [[Hardware/Storage/03 — NVMe, SATA & PCIe]]

**SIMD** (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) — Processing multiple data elements with one instruction. [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture]]

**SMT** (Simultaneous Multithreading) — Running multiple threads on a single CPU core (Intel Hyper-Threading). [[Concurrency/03 — SMT, Affinity & Thread Pools]]

**Stack** — A LIFO memory region used for function calls, local variables, and return addresses. [[System Software/Function Calls/01 — Hardware Mechanics]]

**Syscall** — A request from user space to the kernel for a privileged operation. [[System Software/04 — Syscalls & Kernel]]

## T

**TLB** (Translation Lookaside Buffer) — A cache for virtual-to-physical address translations. [[Hardware/RAM & Virtual Memory/02 — Page Tables & TLB]]

**Thread** — A lightweight unit of execution that shares its address space with sibling threads. [[Concurrency/01 — Processes & Threads]]

**Transistor** — A semiconductor switch; the fundamental building block of digital circuits. [[Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]]

**Two's Complement** — The standard representation for signed integers where the most significant bit has negative weight. [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness]]

## U

**UEFI** — Unified Extensible Firmware Interface, the modern replacement for BIOS. [[Hardware/10 — Boot Process — BIOS, UEFI & Bootloader]]

## V

**Virtual Memory** — An abstraction that gives each process its own address space, backed by physical RAM and disk. [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]]

## Exercises

1. Pick 3 unfamiliar terms from this glossary. For each, read the linked note, then explain the term to an imaginary colleague in your own words.

2. **Categorization:** Group 10 terms into these categories: CPU, Memory, Concurrency, I/O. Some terms belong in multiple categories.

3. **Term chain:** Choose two terms and explain how they relate. Example: "TLB is a cache for page table entries — without it, every memory access would require walking the page table." Write 3 such chains.

Learning Path \u2014 Computer Systems:
# Computer Systems — Learning Path

> Main Path = the core sequence. Deep Dives = go deeper on specific topics when you're ready. Chapter Summaries = connect the dots after completing a section.

## Foundation

Start with the big picture — how a transistor becomes a running program — then use the Glossary as your reference companion throughout.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| — | [[Glossary|Glossary]] | Reference — consult as needed |

---

## Main Path — The Core Sequence

Follow these in order. Each note builds on the previous one.

### Hardware (01–15)

From a single transistor holding charge to a CPU executing billions of instructions per second. You'll learn: how bits and bytes store numbers in real capacitors, two's complement and endianness, assembly language (the CPU's native tongue — MOV, ADD, CMP, JMP), CPU architecture (pipeline stages, superscalar execution, branch prediction, out-of-order execution), memory hierarchy (registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → SSD — each level 10-100× slower than the last), and memory allocation basics. After this section you understand what your code actually runs on.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| 01 | [[Hardware/01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems|Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] | ✓ |
| 02 | [[Hardware/02 — Negative Numbers & Endianness|Negative Numbers & Endianness]] | ✓ |
| 03 | [[Hardware/03 — Assembly Language|Assembly Language]] | ✓ |
| 04 | [[Hardware/04 — CPU Architecture|CPU Architecture]] | ✓ |
| 05 | [[Hardware/05 — Memory Hierarchy|Memory Hierarchy]] | ✓ |

### Memory & Storage (06–08)

How malloc/free actually work (free lists, coalescing, fragmentation), virtual memory (the greatest lie in computing — every process thinks it owns all RAM), and RAID. The deep dives below cover the full RAM/VM and storage hardware stacks.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| 06 | [[Hardware/06 — Memory Allocation|Memory Allocation]] | ✓ |
| 07 | [[Hardware/07 — Virtual Memory — The Full Picture|Virtual Memory — The Full Picture]] | ✓ |
| 08 | [[Hardware/08 — RAID|RAID]] | ✓ |

### I/O & Peripherals (09–12)

How data moves between the CPU and the outside world. DMA (peripherals write to RAM directly), boot process (BIOS/UEFI → bootloader → kernel), GPU architecture (SIMD, warps, memory bandwidth), and interrupts & exceptions.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|

| 10 | [[Hardware/10 — Boot Process — BIOS, UEFI & Bootloader|Boot Process — BIOS, UEFI & Bootloader]] | ✓ |
| 11 | [[Hardware/11 — GPU Architecture|GPU Architecture]] | ✓ |
| 12 | [[Hardware/12 — Interrupts & Exceptions|Interrupts & Exceptions]] | ✓ |

### Virtualization & Security (13–15)

Hypervisors, containers, kernel modules, and hardware security (Spectre, Meltdown, TPM, SGX).

| # | Note | Quiz |

| 14 | [[Hardware/14 — Kernel Modules & Drivers|Kernel Modules & Drivers]] | ✓ |
| 15 | [[Hardware/15 — Hardware Security|Hardware Security]] | ✓ |

### System Software (16–23)

The software layer between hardware and your program. Compiler pipeline, linking, syscalls, system-level I/O, performance optimization, signals, and memory bugs.

The hardest problem in systems programming. Processes vs threads, the OS scheduler, thread pools, data races, deadlock, atomics, lock-free programming, and practical concurrency patterns.

Learning Path \u2014 C++:
# C++ — Learning Path

> Follow the phases in order. Each phase builds on the previous one. Notes within each phase are sequential.

## Phase 1 — Primer (01 — Primer/)

Absolute beginner entry point. You'll learn what a variable IS at the hardware level — capacitors holding charge mapped to names via the compiler — not just `int x = 5`. Covers the stack (RSP subtract = allocation, one CPU instruction), control flow (how `if`/`for` change RIP), functions (call/ret, stack frames, calling conventions), arrays in contiguous memory, pointers as typed addresses, and the full compile→link→run pipeline. After this phase you can read any basic C++ program and trace its execution from source text to CPU instructions. No quizzes here — just type every example and complete every exercise.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 01 | [[01 — Primer/01 — Variables, Types & Memory|Variables, Types & Memory]] | ✓ |
| 02 | [[01 — Primer/02 — Control Flow|Control Flow]] | ✓ |
| 03 | [[01 — Primer/03 — Functions & The Call Stack|Functions & The Call Stack]] | ✓ |
| 04 | [[01 — Primer/04 — Arrays, Vectors & Loops|Arrays, Vectors & Loops]] | ✓ |
| 05 | [[01 — Primer/05 — Pointers & References|Pointers & References]] | ✓ |
| 06 | [[01 — Primer/06 — Compiling & Running Programs|Compiling & Running Programs]] | ✓ |


## Phase 2 — Syntax & Types (02 — Syntax & Types/)

C++-specific syntax that doesn't exist in C or other languages. Enums and enum class (why the C++11 version is strictly safer), references as non-nullable aliases, the many meanings of `static` (file-local, class-scope, thread-local, function-persistent), structured bindings for destructuring tuples and structs, type deduction rules (`auto`, `decltype`, `decltype(auto)`), implicit vs explicit conversions and the `explicit` keyword, const correctness (the most important keyword in C++ for preventing bugs), the preprocessor and macros (and why modern C++ replaces most of them), string types (`std::string`, `std::string_view`, C-strings, SSO), `std::format` (type-safe, locale-aware replacement for `printf` and string streams), and a deep dive into templates (SFINAE, `enable_if`, dependent names, two-phase lookup, nontype params, template template params). After this phase you can read any C++ syntax with confidence.

| 02 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/02 — References|References]] | ✓ |
| 03 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/03 — static in C++|static in C++]] | ✓ |
| 04 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/04 — Structured Bindings|Structured Bindings]] | ✓ |
| 05 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/05 — Type Deduction|Type Deduction]] | ✓ |

| 07 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/07 — const Correctness|const Correctness]] | ✓ |

| 09 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/09 — String Types & string_view|String Types & string_view]] | ✓ |
| 10 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/10 — std format (C++20)|std format (C++20)]] | ✓ |
| 11 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/11 — Initialization Rules|Initialization Rules]] | ✓ |

| 13 | [[02 — Syntax & Types/13 — Templates Deep Dive|Templates Deep Dive]] | ✓ |


## Phase 3 — Memory (03 — Memory/)

C++'s defining feature: you control where every object lives. Stack vs heap (one instruction vs ~50-200+ cycles), object memory layout (vtable pointers, padding, alignment, cache-line effects), smart pointers (`unique_ptr` = exclusive ownership with zero overhead, `shared_ptr` = atomic reference counting with control blocks, `weak_ptr` = cycle-breaking observer), RAII (the single most important C++ idiom — constructors acquire, destructors release, exceptions can't leak), `std::array` vs C arrays (bounds safety, `.size()`, no decay to pointer), `std::vector` internals (capacity doubling, amortized O(1), iterator invalidation, SSO for strings), `std::map`/`unordered_map` (red-black tree vs hash table, key requirements, collision resolution), iterators and algorithms (the STL's separation of data from operations), `std::span` (non-owning view over contiguous data), `std::filesystem` (portable path manipulation), the full container ecosystem (`deque`, `set`, `stack`, `queue`, `priority_queue`, `std::pmr`), and the STL algorithm catalog (sorting, binary search, heap operations, permutations, numeric, memory). After this phase you understand how memory works and can solve 90% of data manipulation tasks without writing a single loop.

| 13 | [[03 — Memory/13 — STL Algorithms Deep Dive|STL Algorithms Deep Dive]] | ✓ |


## Phase 4 — OOP & Generics (04 — OOP & Generics/)

The two pillars of C++ abstraction: runtime polymorphism (inheritance + virtual functions) and compile-time polymorphism (templates). Inheritance and virtual dispatch (vtables, vptr, the cost of a virtual call vs static dispatch), lambdas and function objects (closures, captures by value/reference, `std::function`'s type-erasure cost), C++ casting (`static_cast`, `dynamic_cast`, `const_cast`, `reinterpret_cast` — when each is correct and when each is a bug) plus RTTI, operator overloading (making your types feel like built-ins — but don't abuse it), and templates + concepts (compile-time code generation, SFINAE, fold expressions, C++20 concepts as type constraints). After this phase you can design abstractions that are both type-safe and zero-overhead.

| # | Note | Quiz |

| 03 | [[04 — OOP & Generics/03 — C++ Casting & RTTI|C++ Casting & RTTI]] | ✓ |
| 04 | [[04 — OOP & Generics/04 — Operator Overloading|Operator Overloading]] | ✓ |
| 05 | [[04 — OOP & Generics/05 — Templates & Concepts|Templates & Concepts]] | ✓ |
| 06 | [[04 — OOP & Generics/06 — Multiple Inheritance|Multiple Inheritance]] | ✓ |


## Phase 5 — Modern C++ (05 — Modern C++/)

C++11/14/17/20/23 features that change how you write code. Move semantics and perfect forwarding (rvalue references, `std::move`, `std::forward`, the rule of five vs rule of zero), `constexpr`/`consteval`/`constinit` (moving computation to compile time — faster runtime, caught-at-build errors), `noexcept` and its interaction with move semantics (why vector grows by copying when moves aren't noexcept), sum types (`std::variant` replaces unions, `std::optional` replaces sentinel values, `std::any` for true type erasure), coroutines (stackless, suspendable functions — the foundation of C++20 async), `std::ranges` (composable, lazy, pipeable algorithms — no more `begin()`/`end()` boilerplate), the C++23 overview (deducing `this`, `std::expected`, monadic operations, `if consteval`), value categories (lvalue, rvalue, xvalue, prvalue, glvalue — the taxonomy that explains every reference binding rule), and type deduction deep dive (template deduction's three cases, `decltype` with parenthesized names, `decltype(auto)`, universal reference overloading traps). After this phase you write C++ the way it's meant to be written today, not 1998.

## Phase 6 — Bridge to Unreal Engine (06 — Bridge/)

Connecting standard C++ to real-world development. C++ vs UE (why UE has its own containers, macros, GC, and reflection), the full compile pipeline, C++20 modules, exceptions and error handling, CMake & build systems, build & debug tools (compiler flags, sanitizers, profilers), the C++ concurrency API (`std::thread`, `std::mutex`, `std::future`, `std::async`), performance optimization (profiling methodology, cache-conscious design, branch prediction, SIMD, allocators), C & C++ interoperability (`extern "C"`, opaque handles, ABI boundaries), the C++ toolchain (vcpkg, clang-tidy, clang-format, CI/CD, Compiler Explorer), and concurrent data structures (thread-safe stack/queue/hash-map, lock-free Treiber stack, ABA problem). After this phase you can work in any C++ codebase, engine or not.

## Phase 7 — Software Design (07 — Software Design/)

Turning C++ mechanisms into maintainable systems. SOLID principles (SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP), design patterns (Strategy, Observer, Command, Adapter, Singleton, Visitor, CRTP, Bridge, Type Erasure, Prototype, Decorator, Flyweight, Template Method, State, Facade), dependency management (Pimpl, self-sufficient headers, ADL, ODR), and testing & quality (Catch2, unit/integration/E2E testing, TDD, sanitizer CI pipeline). After this phase you can design C++ systems that survive years of feature work — and prove they work with automated tests.

Learning Path \u2014 Game Math:
# Game Math — Learning Path

> Each section is a self-contained module. Foundation is the only required prerequisite — after that, pick whichever section matches what you're building. Within each section, follow the listed order.

## Foundation — Required Prerequisite

The three pillars of 3D math. Trigonometry & Vectors (dot product = projection, cross product = perpendicular vector — the two operations that power all of 3D graphics), Matrices & Coordinate Systems (4×4 homogeneous matrices, column-major storage, model→world→view→clip transforms), and Quaternions & 3D Rotations (4D math that avoids gimbal lock, interpolates smoothly via slerp).

Start with the full-stack overview to see how these connect, then dive into each.

| #   | Note                                      | Quiz                             |                       |
| --- | ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | --------------------- |
| —   | [[From Trigonometry to 3D Transformations | From Trig to 3D — Full Picture]] | Overview — read first |
| 1   | [[Trigonometry & Vectors                  | Trigonometry & Vectors]]         | ✓                     |
| 2   | [[Matrices & Coordinate Systems           | Matrices & Coordinate Systems]]  | ✓                     |
| 3   | [[Quaternions & 3D Rotations              | Quaternions & 3D Rotations]]     | ✓                     |

---

## Module: Physics Simulation

Making objects move and collide realistically. Rigid body dynamics (F=ma, torque, inertia tensors), numerical integration (Euler, semi-implicit Euler, Verlet, RK4), constraints & contact (joints, impulse-based resolution), and continuous collision detection (preventing tunneling). Start with the Overview for the big picture.

| #   | Note                                                            | Quiz                             |                       |
| --- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | --------------------- |
| —   | [[Physics/Collision Detection/00 — Collision Detection Overview | Collision Detection Overview]]   | Overview — read first |
| 1   | [[Physics/Simulation/01 — Rigid Body Dynamics                   | Rigid Body Dynamics]]            | ✓                     |
| 2   | [[Physics/Simulation/02 — Numerical Integration]]               | Numerical Integration]]          | ✓                     |
| 3   | [[Physics/Simulation/03 — Constraints & Contact                 | Constraints & Contact]]          | ✓                     |
| 4   | [[Physics/Simulation/04 — Continuous Collision Detection        | Continuous Collision Detection]] | ✓                     |

## Module: Collision Detection

Determining whether two shapes overlap — the computational core of any physics engine. AABB/sphere tests, ray casting, Separating Axis Theorem, and GJK/EPA (the general algorithm for arbitrary convex shapes).

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| 1 | [[Physics/Collision Detection/01 — AABB and Sphere|AABB & Sphere]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[Physics/Collision Detection/02 — Ray Casting|Ray Casting]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Physics/Collision Detection/03 — Separating Axis Theorem|Separating Axis Theorem]] | ✓ |
| 4 | [[Physics/Collision Detection/04 — GJK Algorithm|GJK Algorithm]] | ✓ |

## Module: Animation

Making characters move. Interpolation & Bezier splines (lerp, smoothstep, cubic Bezier, Catmull-Rom), skeletal animation (bones, skinning matrices, inverse kinematics), and slerp & kinematics (spherical linear interpolation for quaternion blending). Start with the Curves Overview.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| — | [[Animation/Curves & Interpolation/00 — Curves Overview|Curves Overview]] | Overview — read first |
| 1 | [[Animation/Curves & Interpolation/01 — Interpolation, Bezier & Splines|Interpolation, Bezier & Splines]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[Animation/Skeletal Animation/01 — Skeletal Animation|Skeletal Animation]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Animation/Skeletal Animation/02 — Slerp & Kinematics|Slerp & Kinematics]] | ✓ |

## Module: Navigation

How characters find their way through a 3D world. A* pathfinding, steering behaviors, and navmesh generation (Recast/Detour internals).

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| 1 | [[Navigation/01 — A Star & Pathfinding|A* & Pathfinding]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[Navigation/02 — Steering Behaviors|Steering Behaviors]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Navigation/03 — Navmesh Generation|Navmesh Generation]] | ✓ |

## Module: Rendering

How 3D math becomes pixels on screen. Projection & clip space, barycentric coordinates, BRDF & lighting models, spherical harmonics, Monte Carlo & path tracing, color spaces & HDR, signed distance fields, and voxels & octrees.

| # | Note | Quiz |

## Module: Procedural Generation

Generating content algorithmically. Noise generation (Perlin, simplex, Worley), sampling & placement (Poisson disc, blue noise), L-systems & marching cubes, and probability & randomness.

Frequency domain & FFT, spatial audio (HRTF, Doppler effect), and network interpolation (dead reckoning, extrapolation).

## Capstone: Geometric Algebra

A unified mathematical framework that subsumes vectors, quaternions, complex numbers, and projective geometry into a single algebra. Take after mastering Foundation.

Connecting pure math to Unreal Engine's implementation. FVector/FRotator/FQuat, FTransform & FMatrix, FMath, collision & intersection math, and curves/ranges/random.

Learning Path \u2014 Unreal Engine:
# UE Core — Learning Path

> Follow the Main Path in order. Each section builds on the previous one. The full-stack pipeline overview is optional but recommended before starting.

## Optional Prerequisite — The Big Picture

| #   | Note                                                   | Quiz                  |                                                         |
| --- | ------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| —   | [[From UCLASS to Running Object — The UE Core Pipeline | Full-Stack Pipeline]] | Overview — how UHT, reflection, GC, and objects connect |

---

## Main Path

### 1. Core Systems (1–4)

The foundation every UE class sits on. UHT, macros & reflection (how `UCLASS()`/`UPROPERTY()`/`UFUNCTION()` feed the Unreal Header Tool), UE inheritance & UObject system (why everything inherits from UObject), pointer ecosystem (TObjectPtr, TSharedPtr, TWeakObjectPtr, TSoftObjectPtr), and FName/FString/FText.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| 1 | [[UHT, Macros & Reflection|UHT, Macros & Reflection]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[UE Inheritance & UObject System|UE Inheritance & UObject System]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Pointer Ecosystem|Pointer Ecosystem]] | ✓ |
| 4 | [[FName vs FString vs FText|FName vs FString vs FText]] | ✓ |

### 2. Containers (5–8)

UE's custom container library — why it exists, how it differs from STL. TArray, TMap, TSet internals, and UE templates & T-containers.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|------|------|
| 5 | [[TArray Internals|TArray Internals]] | ✓ |
| 6 | [[TMap Internals|TMap Internals]] | ✓ |
| 7 | [[TSet Internals|TSet Internals]] | ✓ |
| 8 | [[UE Templates & T-Containers|UE Templates & T-Containers]] | ✓ |

### 3. Casting & Types

Type conversion in UE's reflection system. Why `Cast<T>()` replaces `dynamic_cast` (1-2 cycles via bitwise flag check instead of 50-200+ via vtable walk), interface casting, and pitfalls.

| #   | Note                                                              | Quiz                             |                       |
| --- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | --------------------- |

| 2   | [[Language & Patterns/Casting/02 — Interface Casting & References | Interface Casting & References]] | ✓                     |
| 3   | [[Language & Patterns/Casting/03 — When to Use & Pitfalls         | When to Use & Pitfalls]]         | ✓                     |

### 4. Advanced (9–10)

Edge cases and modern patterns. Move semantics in UE (how UE handles move with its own types), and UE enums & flags (UENUM, EClassCastFlags).

| #   | Note                   | Quiz                   |     |
| --- | ---------------------- | ---------------------- | --- |
| 9   | [[Move Semantics in UE | Move Semantics in UE]] | ✓   |
| 10  | [[UE Enums & Flags     | UE Enums & Flags]]     | ✓   |
Learning Path \u2014 Unreal Engine Netwo:

> Follow the Main Path in order. The full-stack pipeline overview is optional but recommended before starting.

## Main Path

### 1. Foundation (1–4)

The client-server model in Unreal Engine. Network roles (Authority, Autonomous Proxy, Simulated Proxy), RPCs (Server/Client/NetMulticast), RepNotifies & OnRep (automatic property replication with callback hooks), and PushModel & dirty marking (UE5's optimization).

### 2. Iris — UE5's New Replication System (5–6)

UE5's ground-up rewrite of replication. Better CPU cache behavior, data-oriented design, per-object filtering, and configurable replication graphs.

### 3. Advanced (7)

The deepest layer. GAS networking & prediction (how the Gameplay Ability System handles networked abilities — client prediction, server reconciliation, rollback).

Learning Path \u2014 Algorithms & Comple:
# Algorithms & Complexity — Learning Path

## Foundations

The language of algorithmic analysis. The Big O complexity spectrum (O(1) → O(log n) → O(n) → O(n log n) → O(n²) → O(2ⁿ) — what each means, how to recognize them in real code, and why the difference between O(n²) and O(n log n) is the difference between "runs in 1 second" and "runs in 30 years"), amortized analysis & real profiling (why `vector::push_back` is O(1) amortized — geometric growth means each element is copied at most twice on average — and how to profile real C++ code to verify theoretical complexity with `perf` and timing), and applied examples comparing raw C++ vs Unreal Engine (the same algorithm in std::vector vs TArray, std::sort vs TArray::Sort — theoretical complexity is the same, but constant factors differ dramatically due to allocators, cache behavior, and engine overhead). After this section you can read any algorithm and tell whether it will scale.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum|The Big O Complexity Spectrum]] | ✓ |
| 2 | [[Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data|Amortized Analysis & Real Profiling Data]] | ✓ |
| 3 | [[Applied Examples - Raw C++ vs Unreal Engine|Applied Examples — C++ vs UE]] | ✓ |

## Classic Algorithms

Three algorithm families every game developer encounters. Sorting algorithms (bubble, insertion, merge, quick, heap, intro — why std::sort uses Introsort, a hybrid of quicksort + heapsort + insertion sort, and when each is cache-friendly), graph algorithms (BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, A* — from pathfinding to dependency resolution, the algorithms that power navigation, build systems, and social graphs), and dynamic programming (memoization, tabulation, optimal substructure — solving hard problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, from inventory systems to animation blending). After this section you have a working toolkit of classic algorithms and know when each applies.

| # | Note | Quiz |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | [[04 — Sorting Algorithms|Sorting Algorithms]] | ✓ |
| 5 | [[05 — Graph Algorithms|Graph Algorithms]] | ✓ |
| 6 | [[06 — Dynamic Programming|Dynamic Programming]] | ✓ |
DeepVault Guide: # DeepVault Guide Welcome to DeepVault — an interactive learning fortress for mastering C++, Unreal Engine, and the computer systems beneath them. ## How This Works Every folder on the dashboard contains **Learn** notes and **Quiz** exercises: | Mode | What It Does | |---|---| | **Learn** | The full vault note, rendered exactly as it appears in Obsidian. Read, scroll the right-side table of contents, click wiki links to jump between notes. | | **Quiz** | 5-10 randomized questions per note — multiple choice and text input. Instant feedback. Scores saved locally on your device. | ### Tracking Your Progress Progress is based on **quiz scores only** — reading a note does not count. Here's how it works: | Status | What It Means | |---|---| | **NOT STARTED** | You haven't taken the quiz yet | | **IN PROGRESS** | At least 1 attempt, but best score is below 80% | | **COMPLETED** | You scored 80% or higher on at least one attempt | The folder header shows your overall completion percentage. Quiz scores and attempt history are saved locally via localStorage — they never leave your device. ### Bookmarks **Bookmark notes** you want to return to — click the ☆ star on any note card in the Study tab. Bookmarked notes appear under the ⭐ Bookmarks tab for quick access. - Click ☆ to bookmark → turns to ⭐ (gold) - Click ⭐ to remove bookmark - Switch to the Bookmarks tab to see only your bookmarked notes - Bookmarks are included in Export/Import — they travel with your progress **Wiki links** like [[04 — CPU Architecture]] connect notes to each other. Click any link to jump to that note's Learn page. **Recent notes** appear on the dashboard below your folders — quick jump back to what you were studying. ### WIP Categories Categories that are still under development show an amber dot badge ● on the dashboard. Hover or long-press for "Work in progress" — these categories have fewer notes but are actively growing. --- ## Master Study Order This vault is designed to be read in order. Each note builds on the ones before it. See the [[C++/Learning Path]] for the full reading order — this is just the big-picture map. ### Layer 1 — Computer Systems (start here) Without understanding the hardware, C++ is just magic words. Start with the physical reality. Covering: bits and bytes → assembly → CPU → memory hierarchy → virtual memory → storage → DMA → networking → boot process → GPU → interrupts → system software → concurrency. Over 50 notes from transistor to thread pool. Start with [[01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Computer Systems|Computer Systems Learning Path]]. ### Layer 2 — C++ Once you understand the hardware, learn the language that runs on it. 52 notes across 6 phases: Primer (absolute beginner) → Syntax & Types → Memory → OOP & Generics → Modern C++ → Engine Bridge. Every note has exercises, See Also, and quizzes. Start with [[01 — Primer/01 — Variables, Types & Memory|Variables, Types & Memory]] and follow the [[Learning Path — C++|C++ Learning Path]]. ### Layer 3 — Game Math The mathematics that make 3D worlds possible. 45 notes across 10 subfolders: Trigonometry → Matrices → Quaternions → Collision Detection → Physics Simulation → Animation → Navigation → Rendering → Procedural Generation → Audio & Networking → Geometric Algebra → UE Bridge. Framework-agnostic (pure C++ structs, not UE-specific). Start with [[From Trigonometry to 3D Transformations]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Game Math|Game Math Learning Path]]. ### Layer 4 — Unreal Engine How UE turns C++ into a game engine. 14 notes: UHT & Macros → Pointer Ecosystem → String Types → Casting → Containers → Templates → Move Semantics. Start with [[UHT, Macros & Reflection]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Unreal Engine|UE Core Learning Path]]. ### Layer 5 — UE Networking Multiplayer from the hardware up. 7 notes: Network Roles → RPCs → RepNotifies → PushModel → Iris Replication → Iris Filtering → GAS Networking. Start with [[Network Roles]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Unreal Engine Networking|UE Networking Learning Path]]. ### Layer 6 — Big O & Algorithms 6 notes: Complexity classes → Amortized analysis → Applied examples → Sorting algorithms → Graph algorithms → Dynamic programming. Start with [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Algorithms & Complexity|Algorithms Learning Path]]. --- ## Reference - [[Glossary]] — Every acronym defined (MMU, TLB, PTE, MESI, PLT, GOT...) - [Learning Paths](#/reference/Learning%20Path) — All six category paths - Use the right-side table of contents to jump between sections in any note - Check the dashboard for recently viewed notes and quiz scores --- DeepVault is built for Mayk's vault. All notes are source-verified against UE 5.8 engine code. ### Moving Between Devices Your quiz progress lives in your browser's localStorage. To continue studying on another device: 1. Export on your current device — downloads a JSON file 2. Transfer the file (email, AirDrop, cloud) 3. Import on the new device — merges all your scores Export and Import buttons are at the bottom of the dashboard. No account needed. ### Cloud Sync Sync your progress across all devices with a free GitHub token. Click **☁️ Sync** at the bottom of the dashboard: 1. Create a **classic token** at [github.com/settings/tokens](https://github.com/settings/tokens) — check ONLY the `gist` scope 2. Paste it into DeepVault and click **Connect** 3. Progress auto-saves to a private GitHub Gist after every quiz and bookmark 4. On another device, paste the same token — all progress auto-loads Your data stays in YOUR GitHub account — private, encrypted, invisible to anyone else. --- Double-click index.html to launch. Everything stays on your device.
